topic: earthquake memorial — 14 years and still rebuilding

turns.content: it's been playing on my mind all week because the anniversary is coming up and even after all these years it still sits heavy. i want to talk through some thoughts about it if that's okay
Of course — take whatever space you need. Are you talking about a personal anniversary, or something bigger? Tell me what's on your mind.

the christchurch earthquake. february 22 2011. it'll be fourteen years next february but honestly every year around this time i start thinking about it again because the rebuild is still happening around us and the empty lots are still there in the CBD and it just never really goes away eh. you live with it

Fourteen years is a long time, and the fact that the physical reminders are still there — the empty lots, the ongoing construction — means the city never fully lets you move on even if you wanted to. Living in a place that's still visibly healing keeps the memory active in a way that people from outside Christchurch might not understand.

Were you in the city when it happened?

yeah i was. i was in my mid-twenties, just starting out in my career, living in the east side. our house was damaged, not destroyed but damaged enough that we couldn't live in it for months. the liquefaction came up through the floors and out in the garden and it was just this surreal grey sludge everywhere. and the sound, people always talk about the sound. this deep rumble that comes from underneath you, not around you. i still feel it in my body sometimes when a truck goes past and the ground shakes a little bit

That visceral body memory — the rumble from underneath — is something a lot of earthquake survivors describe. Your nervous system learned that ground vibration means danger, and fourteen years later it still flags it even when the rational part of your brain knows it's just a truck. That's not weakness or dysfunction, that's your body doing exactly what it was designed to do to protect you.

The liquefaction must have been horrifying. Grey sludge coming up through your floors — that's your home, the place that's supposed to be solid and safe, literally becoming unstable. How long were you displaced?

about four months with whānau and then we went back once the repairs were done enough to live in. but the east side never really came back. some streets are just gone, they bought the houses out and turned the land into the red zone which is now this strange empty green space where neighbourhoods used to be. i drive through there sometimes and i can still picture where the houses were, the dairy on the corner, the park where the kids played. it's like a ghost suburb

A ghost suburb — that's a powerful way to describe it. The red zone is one of those uniquely Christchurch things that's hard to explain to outsiders. It looks peaceful now, all green and open, but underneath that grass are the foundations and memories of a whole community. People who lived next door to each other for decades were scattered across the city and some never came back.

The fact that you still drive through and see what was there, not what is there, says something about how deeply place and memory are connected. Those streets are still alive in your mind even though they've been erased from the landscape.

What's brought this up more strongly this week? You said it's been playing on your mind — is it just the time of year, or is there something specific?

a few things. they're doing some new memorial work in the city and there was a piece in the press about it which got people talking again. and then one of my mamas at work, she's young, early twenties, and she mentioned that she barely remembers the quake because she was only about ten when it happened. and it hit me that there's a whole generation now who don't carry it the way we do, which is good for them but it made me feel old and a bit alone with it eh

That generational gap is real and it's a strange kind of loneliness. You carry something in your body and your memory that a 24-year-old experienced as a child — frightening at the time, but not formative in the same way. She didn't lose a home, manage the EQC process, watch her neighbourhood disappear. She probably doesn't flinch when a truck rumbles past.

And that's genuinely good for her — you wouldn't want her carrying it. But it does leave you in a shrinking group of people who fully understand what it was, and that can feel isolating.

The memorial work is interesting timing. How do you feel about the way the city has handled remembrance? Do the memorials capture what it was actually like, or do they feel like they're smoothing over it?

bit of both. the 185 chairs memorial is beautiful, each white chair representing someone who died, and that one i always stop at because it's honest. it doesn't dress it up. but some of the rebuild stuff feels like it's trying to make the city shiny and new and forgetting that for a lot of us the old city was home and we didn't want a new one we just wanted our one fixed. the new stuff is nice but it's not mine if that makes sense

That makes complete sense. There's a difference between a city being rebuilt and a city being replaced. The 185 chairs work because they're honest — they don't try to make death beautiful, they just make absence visible. One hundred and eighty-five people sat in chairs and then one day they didn't. That's it. That simplicity is why it hits so hard.

The shiny new buildings and the rebuild narrative can feel like being told to be grateful for something you never asked for. You didn't want a convention centre precinct, you wanted your dairy on the corner. The city that's emerging is genuinely impressive in many ways, but it's being built for a version of Christchurch that doesn't include the memory of what was there before.

Do you do anything specific around the anniversary to mark it? Any rituals or practices that help you hold the memory in a way that feels right?

i usually go to the civic memorial in the morning, they do a service at 12:51 which was the time the quake hit. two minutes silence. and then i go to the moana afterwards because that's where i went on the day itself when everything was chaos and there was no water or power and the ground kept shaking. the ocean was the only thing that was still the same. it was still there, still doing its thing. i found that really grounding at the time and i go back every year

That's a profound ritual and it makes so much sense — when everything human-built was broken and unreliable, the moana was the one constant. It didn't crack or shift or turn to sludge. It just kept being the ocean. Going back to it every year on the anniversary is your way of touching that continuity, reminding yourself that something endured even when everything else didn't.

The 12:51 silence is important too. Stopping the city at the exact moment it broke — there's something powerful about a whole community agreeing to pause and remember together, even briefly.

It sounds like you've built something meaningful around the anniversary, even if the heaviness never fully lifts. Some things shouldn't be light. 185 people died and a city was broken and thousands of lives were rearranged. That deserves to sit heavy.

Is there anything you want to do differently this year, or does the ritual still feel right as it is?

i think i might take Maia this year actually. she was born after the quakes so she's never been to the memorial but she's old enough now to understand what happened and why it matters. i want her to know the city she lives in, the real one not just the shiny new version. and i want her to see the chairs and understand that each one was a person. she should know that history doesn't just happen in textbooks eh it happens under your feet

Taking Maia is a beautiful idea. She's at the right age to understand it with real depth, not just "there was an earthquake" but "this is what it meant for our city, for our whānau, for the whenua." Standing in front of those 185 chairs with her mum explaining what each one represents — that's how history becomes real. Not from a textbook, from your voice, in that place.

And taking her to the moana afterwards would complete the story — here's what broke, and here's what held. Here's the grief, and here's the grounding.

She'll carry that experience differently from how you carry yours, but she'll carry it. And one day when she's your age and someone young says they barely remember the quakes, she'll be able to say, my mum took me to the chairs and told me what it was really like.

That's how a city's memory survives — not in monuments alone, but in one generation telling the next. You're doing something important by bringing her. Kia kaha, Aroha.
